questions.text: I prefer to frame requests as invitations rather than directives.
I notice when someone is being left out of a conversation even when it is not obvious.
I focus on getting the right answer rather than on how that answer will land socially.
I find it satisfying to bring order to something that was previously disorganized.
I frequently redefine objectives when I think the current ones are wrong.
I prefer to explore possibilities as they arise rather than commit to a single long-term plan.
I feel that the moral weight I assign to suffering is not an opinion I hold but a conviction that organizes my priorities.
I believe that self-reliance is one of the most important qualities a person can develop.
I believe that obsessing over details can prevent you from seeing the bigger picture.
I tend to think of my possessions as shared resources, not private property.
I believe that my ambitions are a healthy and important part of who I am.
I tend not to detect when a question has a hidden agenda behind it.
I hold the view that emotional detachment during painful situations is not a lack of caring but a different and sometimes superior way of caring.
I hold the view that the most useful thing I can offer in a painful situation is clear, structured thinking rather than an emotional response.
I am inclined to keep options open until the group has weighed in.
I find that the emotional charge of a memory fades quickly, leaving only the facts.
I find that when I'm angry, the anger feels like it's burning through me.
I experience frustration as a mild irritation that fades quickly.
